text: So the procedure of course will start in the belly as we're performing a minimally invasive Ira Lewis resection.
Like all foregut procedures, we start off by taking the gastropathic ligament.
Now prior to doing this and prior to putting in the liver retractor, I do a diagnostic laparoscopy to make sure there are no metastatic deposits.
And if there are, we'll send it for frozen section.
Here we are using the harmonic scalpel to mobilize the hiatus.
This plane is often obliterated and challenging simply because most of our patients have undergone neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy and the planes can be quite challenging.
So we tend not to do too much dissection in the chest to avoid making an inadvertent pleurotomy because if we do that then we get a very floppy left hemidiaphragm
making the operation quite challenging.
So after we've done our hiatal dissection, we're going to begin mobilizing our stomach.
We start well away from the stomach, making sure not to injure the gastro-epiploic artery.
Sometimes we can see it, sometimes we can't, but we begin our dissection well away from it.
Typically we will grab some memento vessels and make a little hole.
This will allow us to get into the lesser sac.
And then we just simply march up using the harmonic scalpel.
Again, we're staying well away from the greater curve.
We always reassess to make sure we're not getting too close to the gastroepiploic artery.
Occasionally, you'll have a high-riding splenic flexure, so you have to be cognizant of that because an inadvertent colotomy can be a bit of a pain to deal with.
We continue our dissection towards the short gastrics.
Here you can continue your dissection a little closer to the greater curvature of the stomach as the gastropiploic has sort of fizzled out at this point.
It's important to take your time here because losing control of a short gastric can cause significant bleeding that is sometimes challenging to deal with.
So again, we always keep our gastropiploic artery in plain sight and then we can continue our dissection towards the pylorus.
Here we are again continuing to take down some of these retrogastric adhesions.
It's important here not to injure the gastroduodenal artery.
Once these adhesions are taken down, you'll have a fantastic view of the anterior surface of the pancreas.
As we continue dissecting the retrogastric adhesions, we can often dissect all the way up to the left gastric lymphovascular bundle.
Here we are just getting a bit more length on our gastric conduit by taking some of those omental vessels.
Once we've mobilized most of the stomach, we then turn our attention to the left gastric
artery.
We've cleared off the peritoneal covering of the vessels.
In order to get around the left gastric artery we do have to mobilize the posterior aspect of the right crust.
Once we've done that we can easily pass our stapler at the base of the left gastric artery and vein.
And here we are using our vascular load to staple it off.
Once our first firing is complete, we have improved exposure and sometimes we have to complete the stapling with one more firing.
We have excellent exposure of the distal esophagus here.
And then we can complete our
stapling of the left gastric bundle.
Using the harmonic we're just finishing off some of those pesky adhesions at the level of the hiatus.
Now we turn our attention to creating the gastric conduit.
Our first firing I do use a gray load because
The fat along the lesser curve tends to bleed a lot and is very vascular.
We aim to create about a four centimeter wide conduit.
You don't want it to be too wide as it could become quite boggy in the chest and if you make it too tight there's a slightly higher chance of a leak.
The next step is really key to ensure that you have a nice long conduit.
As we continue our stapling up
we will grab the apex of the stomach and pull it up into the left upper quadrant.
This prevents the stomach from folding as you complete the last few bites of your conduit.
We do occasionally use our right lower quadrant port to pull the stomach down and often straighten up the conduit to make the stapling a little easier.
The next step is the feeding tube and we put feeding tubes in all our patients.
We usually identify an area in the left lower quadrant.
We identify the spot with a 22 gauge needle and we place a stay suture from the abdominal wall to the anti-mesenteric side of the jejunum.
We usually identify a spot about 30 centimeters distal to the ligament of trites.
We use our endo-stitch to secure it to the abdominal wall.
We use the laparoscopic Barone feeding ginostomy set, which is quite nice.
It usually takes just a few minutes to put a feeding tube in.
Here we have the dilator as well as the feeding tube going in.
We do push about 60 cc of saline into the feeding tube as we advance it.
This is a nice trick to get that feeding tube to travel as distal as possible.
After we've tacked it to the abdominal wall, we then place another anti-volvulus stitch.
just about three or four centimeters distal to the part of the feeding tube that's tacked to the abdominal wall.
Once we've done that, then we can be aggressive in our mediastinal dissection.
We like to dissect as much as we can.
Again, here you can see the planes aren't clear.
The patient has had neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy.
we dissect as high as we can and this often makes the VATS dissection a little easier.
We're getting into the left pleural space and then you'll see in a few seconds the diaphragm starts to become a little floppy.
There's the lung.
If the mediastinal fat is attached to the pleura, we'll take the pleura.
prior to turning the patient on the left lateral decubitus position will suture the conduit to the specimen.
It's important to do this in the anatomic position so that when you pull it up into the chest the staple line is facing you.
The first step is taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament and you can see here a lot of our laparoscopic work is already exposed.
We mobilize the ligament all the way to the inferior pulmonary vein.
In this case I did put a diaphragm stitch in that creates better exposure
and prevents that diaphragm from blocking the camera view.
Then turn our attention to the azagus vein.
We'll mobilize it inferiorly and superiorly and then transect it with a vascular load.
We take down the mediastinal pleura with the harmonic scalpel.
We do take it down all the way to the level of the azicus vein.
That mediastinal dissection is carried down all the way to the level of the hiatus at this point.
Once the pleura is divided, we'll begin our mediastinal lymph node dissection and mobilization of the esophagus.
Here we are working anteriorly, sweeping some of the subcranial nodes into the specimen side.
This can be a very vascular area and simply transecting the airdoesophageals with the harmonic can be
adequate but getting blood into the field at this point can make the operation quite challenging so I tend to clip a lot of them.
Many of them also do contain some lymphatic vessels and I think by being very liberal with clips we can reduce the risk of chylothorax.
We try to do an en bloc lymphadenectomy keeping all the lymph nodes with the specimen.
Sometimes we'll take them out separately.
continue our dissection anteriorly sweeping the esophagus off the pericardium trying to sweep as many nodes as we can into the specimen.
Once about half the esophagus is mobilized I tend to pull up the gastric conduit into the chest as this makes the dissection of the esophagus a little easier
as you have more mobility of the esophagus to move it side to side.
Again, it's important as you pull it up that the staple line be facing you.
Sometimes it does have to be adjusted a bit.
It can spiral occasionally, so that's really important to consider.
We then disconnect the conduit from the esophagus and we continue our very liberal use of clips as we dissect the esophagus medially.
I don't look for the thoracic duct, but if I do see it, I will control it with clips.
Here we are continuing our dissection at the level of the esophagus vein.
And as you can see, because we pull that esophagus up,
that exposure becomes far better.
In this case, I did see the thoracic duct and we clipped that and then transected it.
Once we've mobilized the esophagus approximately as high as we can, we'll then transect the esophagus with a blue load
The stapler will come from the anterior 10mm port.
Most of the time you can get through it with just one firing of the stapler.
We'll then use the Orville stapler to create our anastomosis.
We'll pass the anvil through the mouth using the orogastric tube.
We'll create a small hole on the staple line of the esophagus.
We'll pull that through quite easily.
Once you do get it through, we simply cut the blue string and detach the tube from the anvil.
We do use a Swedish DeBakey to grab the anvil so that we can pull off that orogastric tube easily.
I still put a purse string around the anvil just to make it nice and snug so that we can get two complete donuts when we fire our EEA stapler.
Here we are simply tying it.
As you can see the muscle does retract a bit and that purse string really brings the esophageal wall together.
We then create a gastrotomy along the staple line.
Again, very important to maintain the correct position of the gastric conduit.
Once we open the stomach we have our suction ready because there can be some spillage of gastric contents into the pleural space.
We pass our stapler into the chest and then using the foot going into the sock technique we pull the gastric conduit into the stapler
and then deploy the needle.
You then mate the needle with the anvil.
This can be a little tricky using the Orville.
You do have to sometimes wiggle it into place but if you use that Swedish Debakey to hold the white part of the anvil
it usually mates quite easily.
We pull out the stapler, examine our donuts to make sure that they're complete.
At this point we really pass our NG tube into the gastric conduit.
Here it is going distally.
We can often see the anastomosis here with our 30 degree camera.
We simply close the gastrotomy using one or two firings of the blue load.
If we have spent the time in the abdomen to create the omental flap, we cover the anastomosis with some of it and we'll suture it to the prefrontal fascia.
Thank you for watching.
